comment: Structured general-legal-knowledge primers, one per motion type. Written 2026-07-22 from black-letter law ONLY - no grounding row, exam row, or judge-specific material was read or used. For amendment arms D0 (doctrine-only) and L4D (persona+doctrine). Hashed in FREEZE-2 if those arms are signed.
motion_to_dismiss: Controlling standard (Rule 12(b)(6)): dismissal is proper only if the complaint fails to state a claim that is plausible on its face (Twombly/Iqbal). The court accepts well-pleaded factual allegations as true and draws reasonable inferences for the plaintiff, but disregards legal conclusions and threadbare recitals. Plausibility requires more than possibility; sheer speculation fails. Fraud claims need Rule 9(b) particularity (who, what, when, where, how). The court may consider the complaint, documents integral to it or incorporated by reference, and judicially noticeable facts. Leave to amend is granted liberally (Foman) but may be denied for futility or repeated failure. Practical base rate: federal courts grant a large share of contested 12(b)(6) motions at least in part, especially against conclusory fraud and group-pleading theories; well-pleaded contract and statutory claims with documentary support survive more often.
summary_judgment: Controlling standard (Rule 56(a)): judgment is proper when there is no genuine dispute as to any material fact and the movant is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. The movant bears the initial burden (Celotex); the burden then shifts to the non-movant to point to specific admissible evidence showing a genuine dispute - a mere scintilla or metaphysical doubt is insufficient (Anderson; Matsushita). Evidence is viewed in the light most favorable to the non-movant; the court may not weigh credibility. Self-serving affidavits contradicting prior sworn testimony are discounted (sham-affidavit rule). Partial grants narrowing claims are common. Practical base rate: full grants are less common than partial outcomes; motions isolating one dispositive element with a clean paper record succeed most; motions turning on intent, credibility, or disputed expert inference are usually denied at least in part.
preliminary_injunction_tro: Controlling standard (Winter; 2d Cir. gloss): the movant must show (1) likelihood of success on the merits - or, in the Second Circuit for prohibitory relief, sufficiently serious questions plus a balance of hardships tipping decidedly in its favor; (2) likely irreparable harm absent relief - harm compensable by money damages is not irreparable, and delay in seeking relief undercuts urgency; (3) the balance of equities favors the movant; (4) the public interest favors relief. Mandatory injunctions altering the status quo require a heightened clear-or-substantial showing. TROs additionally require immediacy and, ex parte, Rule 65(b) certifications; security under Rule 65(c) may be required. Practical base rate: emergency relief is denied more often than granted; loss of a concrete, imminent, non-monetary interest (trade secrets in active use, imminent eviction, ongoing confusion) succeeds most; purely economic or self-inflicted urgency fails.
motion_to_compel_discovery: Controlling standard (Rules 26(b)(1), 37(a)): discovery must be relevant to a claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case, considering importance of the issues, amount in controversy, parties' access and resources, and burden versus benefit. The movant must certify a good-faith meet-and-confer; motions without a genuine conferral are routinely denied without prejudice. Boilerplate objections are disfavored; the resisting party must state objections with specificity and support burden claims. Privilege assertions require a timely, adequate log. Courts have broad discretion and frequently split the difference - compelling narrowed categories, limiting time periods or custodians. Practical base rate: contested motions to compel are granted at least in part more often than denied outright, especially where requests are targeted and conferral is documented; overbroad demands and sanctioned discovery weapons fail.
class_certification: Controlling standard (Rule 23): the movant must prove, by a preponderance with rigorous analysis (Wal-Mart; Comcast), Rule 23(a) numerosity, commonality (a common question capable of classwide resolution), typicality, and adequacy, plus a 23(b) category - typically (b)(3) predominance of common questions over individual ones and superiority of the class device. Damages models must match the liability theory (Comcast). The Second Circuit also requires an ascertainable class defined by objective criteria. Merits are examined only as necessary to the Rule 23 findings. Practical base rate: securities and antitrust classes with uniform conduct and documentary proof certify most often; consumer and employment classes turning on individualized reliance, injury, or defenses face predominance trouble; partial certification of narrowed classes or issues is common.
daubert_expert_exclusion: Controlling standard (FRE 702; Daubert): the proponent must show by a preponderance that the expert is qualified, the testimony rests on sufficient facts, reliable principles and methods, and a reliable application to the facts (fit). Reliability markers: testability, peer review, known error rate, standards, general acceptance. Gatekeeping targets methodology, not conclusions; shaky but admissible testimony is addressed by cross-examination and contrary evidence, reflecting a liberal admissibility bias. Experts may not merely assume liability facts or parrot counsel; damages experts assuming disputed liability inputs are vulnerable. Courts often trim rather than exclude - striking specific opinions while admitting the rest. Practical base rate: outright exclusion of a qualified expert's entire testimony is the minority outcome; partial exclusion (grant in part) is the modal result on contested motions.
